title: Cherry-Pick, Replay, Patch Equivalence, and Backports · Recovery Case: Gita Patch Id Equivalence
summary: A security fix must be backported across three maintained lines where commits differ, one patch is already present under a new ID, and the original series includes a merge. Planned artifact: Add and verify the gita patch id equivalence increment in multi-line backport and patch-equivalence record. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Use patch-id, cherry marks, range-diff, and tree comparisons to detect likely equivalent changes while naming heuristic limits.
Handle merge mainline, empty, redundant, no-commit, signoff, and conflict continuation choices with release-policy evidence.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

starterFiles.shell:
# git-advanced cumulative repository evidence notebook
# Git operations change only disposable simulated repository state.
# Inspect status, diffs, graph, refs, and recovery before every mutation.

steps.title: Predict Gita Patch Id Equivalence
steps.instruction:
theory case — Case G13 is a theory in a disposable repository. one differently named backport is patch-equivalent and should not be replayed twice. The learner must predict minus marks likely equivalent patches while plus marks changes absent from the target, run git cherry -v release security-fix, explain the parser fix is equivalent but the dependency-bound test remains target-specific work, test this changed condition: alter context without changing the logical patch and compare heuristic patch IDs with behavior, and preserve this recovery boundary: tag each target pre-backport tip under refs/recovery before sequencer operations.

Before reading, predict which action preserves the Gita Patch Id Equivalence contract for the supplied data.

content.title: Reasoning move
content.text: Locate repository and operation state, compare working tree, index, refs, and graph, preserve a recovery reference, preview the smallest change, then verify both tree behavior and history topology.

content.title: Worked case pattern
content.text: Case G13 is a worked example in a disposable repository. one differently named backport is patch-equivalent and should not be replayed twice. The learner must predict minus marks likely equivalent patches while plus marks changes absent from the target, run git cherry -v release security-fix, explain the parser fix is equivalent but the dependency-bound test remains target-specific work, test this changed condition: alter context without changing the logical patch and compare heuristic patch IDs with behavior, and preserve this recovery boundary: tag each target pre-backport tip under refs/recovery before sequencer operations. A defensible solution applies Gita Patch Id Equivalence, preserves the governing invariant or value contract, and connects the observed result to multi-line backport and patch-equivalence record.

content.code:
# Advanced Git 2.55 deterministic scenario 4
# before-ref: main=a11ce00 candidate=b00b1e5
# hypothesis: one differently named backport is patch-equivalent and should not be replayed twice
git cherry -v release security-fix
# expected: minus marks likely equivalent patches while plus marks changes absent from the target
# observation: the parser fix is equivalent but the dependency-bound test remains target-specific work
# changed-case: alter context without changing the logical patch and compare heuristic patch IDs with behavior
# after-ref: main=a11ce00 candidate=b00b1e5
# tree-proof: compare the exact affected tree and paths before judging success
# behavior-proof: retain a changed-case test or invariant that can falsify the Git-only conclusion
# recovery: tag each target pre-backport tip under refs/recovery before sequencer operations
# transfer-limit: patch equivalence is heuristic and each supported runtime needs changed-platform tests
content.caption: Runnable repository-state trace example; predict its result before running, then change one boundary input.

steps.title: Build Gita Patch Id Equivalence evidence
steps.instruction: Append an Advanced Git evidence block headed "# Evidence: gita-patch-id-equivalence-1x0vv4" with exact before and after refs; a falsifiable prediction; the bounded simulator command; expected, observed, tree, behavior, changed-case, and recovery evidence; and one honest real-repository transfer limit.
steps.why: Building a runnable or mechanically reviewable artifact turns Gita Patch Id Equivalence into observable performance evidence.

content.code:
# Evidence: gita-patch-id-equivalence-1x0vv4
# scenario-key: trace-1x0vv4 probe-4vv0x1 boundary-0vv41x owner-1x0vv4x fault-1x0vv4z
# before-ref: main=a11ce1x candidate=b00b1v4
# hypothesis: one differently named backport is patch-equivalent and should not be replayed twice
git cherry -v release security-fix
# expected: minus marks likely equivalent patches while plus marks changes absent from the target
# observation: the parser fix is equivalent but the dependency-bound test remains target-specific work
# changed-case: alter context without changing the logical patch and compare heuristic patch IDs with behavior
# after-ref: main=a11ce1x candidate=c0ffee4
# tree-proof: the affected tree and path set match the predicted repository transition
# behavior-proof: a changed input preserves the named product or release invariant
# recovery: tag each target pre-backport tip under refs/recovery before sequencer operations
# transfer-limit: patch equivalence is heuristic and each supported runtime needs changed-platform tests
